promoted.why: Missing validation of bridge input parameters (replay/proof validation in a bridge handler) is a missing input validation issue at the bridge layer, matching the ground-truth category.

This finding captures the same root cause: exploitation of user token approvals through a mechanism (delegatecall) that allows unauthorized use of approved tokens, matching the ground-truth approval_exploitation vulnerability.
Unvalidated route registration captures the same root cause as faulty route validation—insufficient validation of bridge routes that enables attacks.

approval_drain directly matches approval_exploitation (exploiting user token approvals), and arbitrary_token_listing describes the mechanism of allowing unauthorized token operations through approvals.
Both describe arbitrary external calls with user-supplied data; approval_drain is a specific instantiation of the arbitrary_external_call vulnerability mechanism.

Missing slippage protection is the root-cause mechanism that enables flash loan price manipulation attacks, as it allows attackers to execute large trades without price protection and then manipulate prices via flash loans.
Missing message source validation is synonymous with missing gateway origin check—both refer to failure to verify the sender/origin of cross-chain messages.
Weak source authentication is a synonym for unauthenticated message handler — both describe insufficient validation of caller/source identity before granting privileged execution.
Both refer to governance/admin paths lacking proper validation mechanisms; the finding captures the weak authentication that allows unauthorized reassignment of mint authority.
Finding 0 directly names 'arbitrary_external_call', which is the exact root-cause mechanism of the ground-truth vulnerability (bridge allowing arbitrary external calls with user-supplied calldata).
Both refer to the same root cause: callback execution without validating attacker-supplied parameters (specifically the token argument), enabling exploitation through unvalidated callback inputs.
This finding directly addresses missing input validation, which is the core mechanism of the ground-truth vulnerability concerning insufficient validation of bridge input parameters.
Approval drain with arbitrary call is a compound name describing the same root-cause mechanism: arbitrary external calls with user-supplied calldata that can drain approvals/funds.
Both refer to the same root cause: flashloan callback parameters are executed without validation, allowing attacker-controlled behavior.
Solvency_check_bypass describes bypassing/skipping solvency checks, which is the same root-cause mechanism as missing_solvency_check that enables undercollateralized debt.
state_flag_corruption captures the same root cause as state_flag_reset: improper manipulation of a security flag that bypasses intended checks, though using a broader term.

This finding directly matches the event_spoofing vulnerability: an attacker forging a deposit event (unauthenticated memo) that an off-chain observer trusts to trigger a payout is the exact mechanism described in the ground-truth issue.
Finding 1 directly matches the ground-truth arbitrary_external_call vulnerability; 'approval_drain' is a specific manifestation/consequence of arbitrary external calls with user-supplied calldata.
Both refer to the same root cause: improper whitelist allowing arbitrary calls to token contracts that can invoke transferFrom, enabling approval/token drainage.
Infinite approval standing allowance is a direct mechanism enabling approval exploitation—it allows a bridge to retain and exploit user token approvals indefinitely without restriction.
